title: Unexplained Severe Gynecomastia in a Prepubertal Boy — The Gonadotropin Paradox and an Estradiol Assay Discrepancy
sourceFile: user-provided content (chat) — resident training module (Master Divyanshu Pandey, May–July 2026)

items.title: How to read this case — severe prepubertal gynecomastia with a biochemical paradox
items.content: Master Divyanshu Pandey, 12 years old, was seen over three visits (May 3 – July 5, 2026) for severe, huge bilateral gynecomastia. Genital examination shows a prepubertal state: testicular volume 2 mL (right) and 1 mL (left), stretched penile length 4.5 cm. Testosterone is profoundly low at 0.1 ng/mL. Case notes document two wildly different estradiol values — 685 pmol/L on June 6 and 21 pg/mL elsewhere — while gonadotropins remain measurable and inappropriately 'normal' for the supposed estrogen load (LH 2.4, FSH 4.7 mIU/mL). The fellow's first task is not to pick a diagnosis from the breast size alone, but to decide whether this boy has true hyperestrogenism or an assay artefact masquerading as one — because the management fork (aggressive tumour hunt vs. careful re-verification) depends entirely on that answer.
keyPoints: 12y boy, prepubertal testes (1–2 mL), severe bilateral gynecomastia
T 0.1 ng/mL; documented E2 either 685 pmol/L or 21 pg/mL — a critical discrepancy
LH 2.4 / FSH 4.7 are not suppressed despite the purported high E2 — the central paradox

items.title: Why prepubertal testicular volume reframes the entire differential
items.content: The orchidometer is the fastest bedside discriminator. Central (gonadotropin-driven) precocious puberty enlarges the testes (>4 mL) because LH/FSH stimulate Leydig and Sertoli cells. Exogenous estrogen, aromatase excess, and estrogen-secreting tumours suppress the HPG axis and leave testes prepubertal (<2–4 mL) even as breast tissue grows. This boy's 1–2 mL testes with florid gynecomastia therefore exclude gonadotropin-driven central puberty as the explanation for the breast development and shift the differential toward an estrogen source that is not LH-driven testicular testosterone: covert exogenous exposure, adrenal steroidogenic pathology (including CAH variants), peripheral aromatase excess, or a micro estrogen-secreting testicular tumour. Palpate to confirm true glandular parenchyma (firm, retroareolar, concentric) rather than lipomastia before committing to an endocrine work-up.
keyPoints: Testes >4 mL → think central precocious puberty; testes 1–2 mL + gynecomastia → not CPP
Prepubertal testes with severe breast tissue ⇒ exogenous estrogen / tumour / aromatase excess / adrenal source
Confirm glandular tissue vs fat by palpation first

items.title: Why measurable LH and FSH argue against a true estradiol of 685 pmol/L
items.content: If estradiol were genuinely at 685 pmol/L (~187 pg/mL — see unit note below), it would exert potent negative feedback on the hypothalamus and pituitary, suppressing LH and FSH to near-undetectable levels, exactly as seen in pe-cr-013 (E2 296 pg/mL with LH 0.61, FSH 1.92). Here LH 2.4 and FSH 4.7 are prepubertal-to-early-pubertal and not suppressed at all — physiologically incompatible with sustained tissue-level hyperestrogenism at ~187 pg/mL. The gonadotropin pattern therefore corroborates the lower estradiol value (21 pg/mL) and raises strong suspicion that 685 pmol/L is an immunoassay artefact (cross-reactivity, heterophile antibodies, or a reporting/unit error) rather than the clinical reality. Treat the patient, not the outlier number — but verify before closing the file.
keyPoints: True E2 ~685 pmol/L (~187 pg/mL) should profoundly suppress LH/FSH
LH 2.4 / FSH 4.7 are incompatible with sustained hyperestrogenism at that level
The gonadotropin pattern supports the lower E2 and flags the high value as likely artefact

items.title: How to deconstruct the estradiol discrepancy — units, immunoassay traps, and LC-MS/MS
items.content: Fellows must fluently convert estradiol units: 685 pmol/L × (272.4 g/mol) ÷ 1000 ≈ 187 pg/mL, whereas 21 pg/mL ≈ 77 pmol/L — an order-of-magnitude apart. Automated estradiol immunoassays are calibrated for adult-female ranges and are notoriously unreliable in prepubertal and pubertal males: cross-reactivity with other steroids and metabolites, heterophile-antibody interference, and hook-effect at extreme values can produce falsely elevated or discordant results. A single immunoassay estradiol must never drive invasive imaging or treatment in a boy. The gold standard for verification is liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS, ideally ultrasensitive), which should be used for the repeat draw ordered at the July 5 visit. If LC-MS/MS confirms prepubertal-range estradiol (~20 pg/mL), the work-up pivots toward explaining gynecomastia without massive estrogen excess (local factors, early pubertal shift, occult low-level exposure) rather than chasing adrenal or testicular estrogen-secreting tumours for a phantom hormone level.
keyPoints: 685 pmol/L ≈ 187 pg/mL; 21 pg/mL ≈ 77 pmol/L — know the conversion
Immunoassay E2 in boys: cross-reactivity, heterophile antibodies, hook effect — unreliable
Repeat by LC-MS/MS before acting; a verified low E2 changes the entire work-up

items.title: Why bone age is the arbiter when estradiol values conflict
items.content: Estrogen — not testosterone — is the dominant driver of skeletal maturation and epiphyseal fusion. Chronic exposure to a true estradiol of ~187 pg/mL would rapidly advance bone age well beyond chronological age (12 years). The pending left hand/wrist X-ray ordered July 5 is therefore not a formality: if bone age matches chronological age (12 years), the 685 pmol/L value is almost certainly an assay artefact and 21 pg/mL represents the clinical reality; if bone age is markedly advanced (e.g. 14–15 years), tissue-level estrogen excess is confirmed regardless of which immunoassay draw looked lower, and urgent source localisation is warranted. Bone age thus adjudicates the central diagnostic fork better than repeating the same flawed immunoassay on the same platform.
keyPoints: Estrogen drives epiphyseal maturation — sustained high E2 advances bone age
BA = chronological age (12y) ⇒ 685 pmol/L likely artefact; BA advanced ⇒ true hyperestrogenism
Bone age adjudicates the E2 discrepancy better than repeating the same immunoassay

items.title: How profoundly low testosterone fits a prepubertal boy — and what it excludes
items.content: Total testosterone 0.1 ng/mL (10 ng/dL) is prepubertal and appropriate for 1–2 mL testes. It confirms the child has not entered gonadotropin-driven puberty and rules out the scenario where gynecomastia arises from peripheral aromatisation of abundant gonadal testosterone (the mechanism of physiological pubertal gynecomastia in mid-puberty boys with T >100 ng/dL). With so little androgen substrate, the breast tissue cannot be explained by 'normal pubertal aromatisation' alone. If estradiol is verified as truly elevated, the estrogen must come from a source other than testicular testosterone aromatisation: exogenous estrogen, adrenal androgen aromatisation (aromatase excess or adrenal tumour), or direct estrogen secretion by a testicular stromal tumour. If estradiol is verified as prepubertal-low, the gynecomastia may represent early glandular proliferation with a disproportionate local estrogen:androgen ratio, idiopathic prepubertal gynecomastia, or a very low-level exposure — and the work-up narrows accordingly.
keyPoints: T 0.1 ng/mL matches prepubertal 1–2 mL testes — not gonadotropin-driven puberty
Cannot be explained by aromatisation of abundant gonadal testosterone
If E2 truly high, source is exogenous / adrenal / aromatase excess / testicular tumour — not normal puberty

items.title: Why covert exogenous estrogen remains the most reversible cause to exclude first
items.content: Even with conflicting estradiol numbers, a meticulous exposure history is mandatory and cheap. Ask all caregivers — explicitly, not under 'medications' — about compounded estradiol creams, bioidentical/anti-aging topical regimens, transdermal estradiol gels and sprays (including gender-affirming hormone therapy in any family member), estrogen-containing supplements, soy/phytoestrogen products, and lavender or tea-tree-oil personal-care items. Probe skin-contact logistics: who applies what, where on the body, hand-washing habits, cuddling or co-sleeping after application. Secondary transfer can produce severe prepubertal gynecomastia with prepubertal testes (see pe-cr-014). If exposure is found and removed, estradiol normalises and breast tissue regresses — making this the highest-yield history in the module. Do not skip it because the estradiol values disagree.
keyPoints: Ask all caregivers about creams, gels, BHRT, GAHT, supplements — not just 'medications'
Skin-contact logistics matter: application site, hand-washing, cuddling/co-sleeping
Most reversible cause — exclude before invasive imaging for a possibly artefactual E2

items.title: How adrenal pathology enters the differential — CAH, 17-OHP, and feminising adrenocortical tumours
items.content: If LC-MS/MS confirms genuine hyperestrogenism, adrenal sources must be evaluated. Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH) variants — particularly forms with impaired cortisol synthesis and shunting of precursors toward androgens and estrogens — can present with gynecomastia and abnormal steroid profiles; the ordered 17-hydroxyprogesterone (17-OHP) assesses the classic 21-hydroxylase blockade pathway. A feminising adrenocortical adenoma or carcinoma (rare in children but dangerous) secretes estrogens autonomously, often with raised adrenal androgens (DHEA-S, androstenedione). The May 3 panel included DHEAS — review that result alongside 17-OHP when available. Adrenal imaging (CT or MRI) follows biochemical confirmation, not an unverified immunoassay spike.
keyPoints: CAH variants can distort adrenal steroidogenesis — 17-OHP screens the classic pathway
Feminising adrenocortical tumours secrete estrogen autonomously; some are malignant
Image the adrenals only after LC-MS/MS confirms true hyperestrogenism

items.title: Why aromatase excess syndrome fits prepubertal gynecomastia with low testosterone
items.content: Aromatase excess syndrome (AES) is a rare autosomal-dominant gain-of-function of CYP19A1 causing excessive peripheral conversion of adrenal androgens (DHEAS, androstenedione) into estrogens. Boys present with prepubertal or peripubertal gynecomastia, accelerated growth, advanced bone age, and — because autonomously generated estrogen suppresses the HPG axis — low gonadotropins and low testosterone. This case shares the prepubertal testes and severe gynecomastia pattern, but the non-suppressed gonadotropins argue against florid AES unless estradiol is verified as low. If LC-MS/MS confirms high estrogens with elevated androgen precursors and no tumour on imaging, CYP19A1 genetic testing confirms AES; treatment is a third-generation aromatase inhibitor (anastrozole or letrozole), not surgery.
keyPoints: AES = CYP19A1 gain-of-function → over-aromatisation of adrenal/gonadal androgens
Classic: prepubertal gynecomastia, advanced BA, suppressed LH/FSH, low T
Confirmed AES → aromatase inhibitor (not tamoxifen); genetics if pattern fits after verified labs

items.title: How micro Sertoli or Leydig cell tumours can hide behind prepubertal testicular volume
items.content: Estrogen-secreting testicular stromal tumours — Sertoli cell tumours (including large-cell calcifying variant linked to Peutz–Jeghers syndrome and Carney complex) and Leydig cell tumours — can be sub-centimetre, non-palpable, yet drive florid hyperestrogenism. Normal testicular volume (1–2 mL) and a normal genital examination do not exclude them. If LC-MS/MS confirms true hyperestrogenism, bilateral high-resolution testicular ultrasound is mandatory to search for occult intratesticular lesions, microlithiasis, or calcification. Inhibin B and AMH may support a Sertoli-cell diagnosis. This imaging should follow biochemical verification, not precede it — chasing a micro-tumour for an artefactual estradiol of 685 pmol/L would expose the child to unnecessary radiation and anxiety.
keyPoints: Sertoli/Leydig tumours can be tiny and non-palpable yet strongly estrogenic
1–2 mL testes do NOT exclude a micro-tumour — scrotal US if E2 verified high
Order imaging after LC-MS/MS confirmation, not for an unverified immunoassay spike

items.title: Why pending SHBG will refine the bioavailable hormone picture
items.content: Total estradiol and testosterone alone do not fully describe tissue exposure: sex-hormone-binding globulin (SHBG) binds both steroids and determines the free (bioactive) fraction. In prepubertal boys SHBG is relatively high, so even a modest total estradiol can carry disproportionate biological effect if free estradiol is elevated — or, conversely, a falsely elevated total estradiol on immunoassay may not reflect free hormone at all. Once SHBG returns, calculate free and bioavailable estradiol and testosterone (or request these directly from a reference laboratory) to complement the LC-MS/MS total values. This is particularly useful if total estradiol is borderline or if gynecomastia persists despite 'normal' totals — the free fraction may still be abnormal.
keyPoints: SHBG binds E2 and T — total levels mislead without the free/bioavailable fraction
High SHBG in prepubertal boys affects interpretation of totals
Calculate free E2/T once SHBG returns, especially if totals are borderline

items.title: Why prolactin was ordered — and why it is unlikely to explain this picture
items.content: Prolactin was added to the July 5 panel. Hyperprolactinaemia can cause gynecomastia (and galactorrhea) by suppressing GnRH pulsatility and lowering testosterone, but it does not typically produce estradiol values in the hundreds of pmol/L range, and it would be expected to suppress LH/FSH more than seen here if prolactin were markedly elevated. A co-secreting prolactinoma is exceedingly unlikely to present as isolated prepubertal gynecomastia with prepubertal testes. Nevertheless, prolactin is a cheap screen that completes the pituitary profile if imaging is ever needed, and it excludes a prolactin-driven hypogonadal component contributing to the low testosterone. A normal prolactin supports moving on; a marked elevation would prompt pituitary MRI — but that is not the leading hypothesis in this case.
keyPoints: Hyperprolactinaemia causes gynecomastia via hypogonadism, not massive E2 elevation
Prolactinoma co-secretion is unlikely here — but prolactin is a cheap pituitary screen
Markedly elevated prolactin would prompt MRI; normal result supports the current fork

items.title: How to use the May 3 comprehensive panel results systematically
items.content: The initial visit (May 3) ordered LH, FSH, estradiol, testosterone, DHEAS, LFT, creatinine, serum beta-hCG, and T3/T4/TSH — a sound first-line screen. Beta-hCG elevation would point to hCG-secreting tumours (germ-cell, hepatoblastoma) stimulating Leydig cells; normal beta-hCG makes that pathway unlikely. Normal TSH/T4 excludes hyperthyroidism as a contributor to gynecomastia. LFT and creatinine screen hepatic and renal causes of altered sex-hormone handling. DHEAS (when resulted) helps separate adrenal androgen excess from isolated gonadal pathology. Fellows should assemble all returned values from the May panel before the July follow-up rather than treating each draw in isolation — the narrative emerges from the pattern, not a single analyte.
keyPoints: May 3 panel: gonadotropins, sex steroids, DHEAS, beta-hCG, thyroid, LFT, creatinine
Normal beta-hCG argues against hCG-secreting tumour; normal thyroid excludes hyperthyroid contribution
Integrate all returned values — the pattern matters more than any single draw

items.title: How the way forward sequences — verify, adjudicate, then localise
items.content: Hold to a strict hierarchy for this unresolved case. (1) Verify: repeat estradiol and testosterone by LC-MS/MS; take a meticulous exogenous-estrogen history in all caregivers; review the full May 3 panel including DHEAS and beta-hCG. (2) Adjudicate: bone-age X-ray — if advanced, true hyperestrogenism is confirmed; if concordant with age 12, the 685 pmol/L is likely artefact and 21 pg/mL is clinical reality. (3) Localise (only if E2 verified high): scrotal ultrasound for micro Sertoli/Leydig tumour; adrenal CT/MRI if adrenal androgens elevated; 17-OHP for CAH; CYP19A1 testing if aromatase-excess pattern; calculate free E2/T once SHBG returns. (4) Treat by cause — remove exposure, excise tumour, aromatase inhibitor for AES — never empiric tamoxifen or surgery for an unverified number. The teaching punchline: the gonadotropin pattern already whispers that the high estradiol may not be real; bone age and LC-MS/MS will speak louder.
keyPoints: Verify (LC-MS/MS + exposure history) → Adjudicate (bone age) → Localise (US/CT/genetics) → Treat by cause
Do not image or operate for an unverified immunoassay estradiol
Gonadotropins + bone age + LC-MS/MS resolve the central fork

items.title: Prepubertal gynecomastia with conflicting estradiol — fellow work-up checklist
items.content: ☐ Confirm true glandular gynecomastia by palpation (vs lipomastia). ☐ Document testicular volume, SPL, pubic-hair stage. ☐ Repeat estradiol + testosterone by LC-MS/MS (not the same immunoassay platform alone). ☐ Left hand/wrist bone-age X-ray. ☐ Meticulous exogenous-estrogen history in all caregivers. ☐ Review May 3 panel: beta-hCG, DHEAS, LFT, TSH. ☐ Pending: SHBG, prolactin, 17-OHP, repeat E2. ☐ If E2 verified high: scrotal US ± adrenal CT/MRI; androstenedione if not done. ☐ If E2 verified low/normal: narrow to local/idiosyncratic causes; observe regression if exposure removed; avoid unnecessary tumour imaging.
keyPoints: LC-MS/MS E2/T + bone age + exposure history = the non-negotiable trio
Image only after biochemical verification of true hyperestrogenism
Checklist prevents both over-investigation (artefact) and under-investigation (missed tumour)

items.question: Why do LH 2.4 and FSH 4.7 mIU/mL make a true estradiol of 685 pmol/L unlikely in this boy?
items.answer: Because estradiol at ~685 pmol/L (~187 pg/mL) exerts potent negative feedback on the hypothalamic–pituitary axis, which should suppress LH and FSH to near-undetectable levels — as seen in confirmed hyperestrogenic states. Measurable, prepubertal-range gonadotropins are physiologically incompatible with sustained tissue-level estrogen at that concentration. The gonadotropin pattern therefore supports the lower documented estradiol (21 pg/mL) and flags 685 pmol/L as likely immunoassay artefact until LC-MS/MS proves otherwise.
keyPoints: High E2 suppresses LH/FSH — non-suppressed gonadotropins contradict true E2 ~685 pmol/L
Pattern matches lower E2 (~21 pg/mL), not the higher value
LC-MS/MS needed to confirm, but gonadotropins already raise suspicion

items.question: How should a fellow respond when two estradiol values in the same case differ by an order of magnitude (685 pmol/L vs 21 pg/mL)?
items.answer: First, convert units (685 pmol/L ≈ 187 pg/mL; 21 pg/mL ≈ 77 pmol/L) to confirm the magnitude of discordance. Second, interpret the gonadotropin pattern — non-suppressed LH/FSH favour the lower value. Third, order estradiol and testosterone by LC-MS/MS on a fresh sample, not a repeat immunoassay on the same platform. Fourth, obtain a bone-age X-ray: advanced bone age confirms tissue-active estrogen excess regardless of which draw looked lower; concordant bone age supports an artefact. Fifth, take a meticulous exogenous-estrogen history before any invasive imaging. Do not start tamoxifen, aromatase inhibitors, or tumour surgery until LC-MS/MS and bone age adjudicate the fork.
keyPoints: Convert units; read gonadotropins; repeat by LC-MS/MS on fresh sample
Bone age adjudicates: advanced = true excess; concordant = likely artefact
No treatment or invasive imaging until verification

items.stem: A 12-year-old boy has severe bilateral gynecomastia, prepubertal testes (1–2 mL), testosterone 0.1 ng/mL, documented estradiol 685 pmol/L, LH 2.4 mIU/mL, and FSH 4.7 mIU/mL. What is the most important immediate inference?
options: The gonadotropin pattern suggests the 685 pmol/L estradiol is likely an assay artefact and must be verified by LC-MS/MS before invasive work-up
The high estradiol confirms an estrogen-secreting testicular tumour requiring urgent orchidectomy
The gonadotropin levels confirm central precocious puberty as the cause of gynecomastia
LH and FSH are irrelevant when estradiol is elevated — proceed directly to adrenal CT
items.explanation: True estradiol at ~685 pmol/L (~187 pg/mL) would suppress LH/FSH profoundly; measurable prepubertal gonadotropins are incompatible with that estrogen load, strongly suggesting assay artefact. LC-MS/MS verification and bone age come before tumour imaging or surgery. Prepubertal testes exclude central precocious puberty, and gonadotropins are central to interpreting conflicting E2 values.

items.stem: Bone-age assessment is ordered in this boy. If the left hand/wrist X-ray shows bone age equal to chronological age (12 years), what is the best conclusion?
options: The 685 pmol/L estradiol is likely an immunoassay artefact; 21 pg/mL is probably the clinical reality — narrow the work-up accordingly
Bone age confirms severe hyperestrogenism regardless of the estradiol values
Bone age is irrelevant when gynecomastia is present — proceed to testicular ultrasound immediately
Concordant bone age proves aromatase excess syndrome
items.explanation: Sustained high estradiol advances bone age rapidly. Concordant bone age at 12 years argues against tissue-level hyperestrogenism at ~187 pg/mL and supports the lower estradiol value as real, making aggressive tumour imaging unnecessary. Bone age is highly relevant here; it does not diagnose aromatase excess (which would show advanced bone age).

items.stem: This boy has testicular volume 2 mL (right) and 1 mL (left) with severe gynecomastia. What does this combination most strongly suggest?
options: The breast development is not driven by gonadotropin-dependent testicular puberty — consider exogenous estrogen, aromatase excess, adrenal pathology, or an estrogen-secreting testicular tumour
Normal physiological pubertal gynecomastia requiring reassurance only
Central precocious puberty with appropriate testicular enlargement
Primary testicular failure with hypergonadotropic hypogonadism
items.explanation: Prepubertal testes (1–2 mL) with florid gynecomastia exclude gonadotropin-driven central puberty (>4 mL testes) and point to an estrogen source independent of LH-driven testicular testosterone. This is not physiological pubertal gynecomastia (which occurs with pubertal testicular volumes and testosterone). Primary testicular failure would show high, not normal, gonadotropins.

items.stem: What is the single most appropriate next laboratory step for the conflicting estradiol results?
options: Repeat estradiol and testosterone by LC-MS/MS on a fresh sample
Repeat the same automated immunoassay estradiol tomorrow
Start empiric anastrozole while awaiting results
Order a karyotype as the first priority
items.explanation: Discordant immunoassay estradiol values in a prepubertal boy demand verification by LC-MS/MS — the gold standard that avoids cross-reactivity and heterophile-antibody artefacts. Repeating the same immunoassay perpetuates the problem. Empiric aromatase inhibition treats an unverified number. Karyotype is not first-line here (prepubertal testes, no dysgenesis features).

items.stem: If LC-MS/MS confirms estradiol ~180 pg/mL with suppressed gonadotropins, what is the correct imaging approach for the testes?
options: Bilateral high-resolution scrotal ultrasound to search for occult Sertoli or Leydig cell tumours despite prepubertal volume
No imaging — testes are too small to harbour a tumour
Immediate bilateral orchidectomy
Abdominal CT alone without scrotal imaging
items.explanation: Estrogen-secreting Sertoli and Leydig cell tumours can be sub-centimetre and non-palpable even with prepubertal testicular volume. Confirmed hyperestrogenism mandates high-resolution scrotal ultrasound. Orchidectomy without localisation is inappropriate; abdominal CT evaluates adrenals but not occult testicular tumours.

items.stem: The family denies the child takes any medications. What is the best next history step?
options: Specifically ask all caregivers about compounded creams, bioidentical/anti-aging topicals, transdermal estradiol gels/sprays (including GAHT), supplements, and skin-contact habits
Accept the denial and proceed to whole-body MRI
Conclude the gynecomastia is idiopathic and discharge
Start tamoxifen empirically
items.explanation: Covert exogenous estrogen transfer from caregivers is common, reversible, and frequently omitted from 'medications' history. The inquiry must be specific and cover all caregivers and contact logistics (see pe-cr-014). Whole-body MRI, idiopathic discharge, or empiric tamoxifen are premature before LC-MS/MS verification and targeted exposure history.

items.statement: In a boy with a true estradiol of 685 pmol/L (~187 pg/mL), LH and FSH would typically remain in the prepubertal normal range.
items.explanation: False. Estradiol at that concentration exerts potent negative feedback on the HPG axis, suppressing LH and FSH to near-undetectable levels. Prepubertal-range gonadotropins (LH 2.4, FSH 4.7) are incompatible with sustained hyperestrogenism at ~187 pg/mL and suggest the high value is an assay artefact.

items.statement: Automated estradiol immunoassays are reliable in prepubertal boys and can be used to guide surgical decisions without LC-MS/MS confirmation.
items.explanation: False. Immunoassays are optimised for adult-female ranges and are unreliable in males and children due to cross-reactivity, heterophile antibodies, and poor accuracy at unusual concentrations. LC-MS/MS must confirm unexpected estradiol before invasive work-up or treatment.

items.statement: If bone age matches chronological age in this boy, it supports the hypothesis that the 685 pmol/L estradiol is an assay artefact.
items.explanation: True. Sustained high estradiol rapidly advances bone age. Concordant bone age at 12 years argues against tissue-level hyperestrogenism at ~187 pg/mL and corroborates the lower estradiol value (21 pg/mL) as the clinical reality.

items.assertion: The non-suppressed LH and FSH in this boy suggest the 685 pmol/L estradiol may not reflect true tissue-level hyperestrogenism.
items.reason: Estradiol exerts potent negative feedback on the hypothalamic–pituitary axis, suppressing gonadotropin secretion when truly elevated.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. Because high estradiol suppresses gonadotropins, the measurable LH/FSH argue against a true E2 of 685 pmol/L and favour assay artefact or the lower value. Hence option 0.

items.assertion: Prepubertal testicular volume (1–2 mL) excludes an estrogen-secreting testicular tumour.
items.reason: Sertoli and Leydig cell tumours can be sub-centimetre and non-palpable yet produce florid hyperestrogenism.
items.explanation: The assertion is false (small tumours can hide behind prepubertal testes) but the reason is true (Sertoli/Leydig tumours can be tiny yet hormonally active). Hence option 3: A is false, R is true.

items.assertion: Bilateral testicular ultrasound should be performed immediately, before LC-MS/MS confirms the estradiol, because gynecomastia is present.
items.reason: Immunoassay estradiol values in prepubertal boys are frequently artefactual and must be verified before invasive imaging.
items.explanation: The assertion is false (imaging should follow LC-MS/MS verification, not precede it) but the reason is true (immunoassay E2 in boys is unreliable). Proceeding to ultrasound on an unverified 685 pmol/L risks over-investigation. Hence option 3.

items.question: List four endogenous causes of severe prepubertal gynecomastia with prepubertal testes (excluding assay artefact).
items.answer: (1) Covert exogenous estrogen exposure (parental creams/gels/BHRT/GAHT transfer). (2) Aromatase excess syndrome (CYP19A1 gain-of-function — peripheral over-conversion of adrenal androgens to estrogens). (3) Estrogen-secreting testicular stromal tumour (micro Sertoli or Leydig cell tumour). (4) Adrenal pathology — feminising adrenocortical tumour or CAH variant with abnormal adrenal steroidogenesis (17-OHP helps screen).

items.question: Convert 685 pmol/L estradiol to pg/mL and state why this matters for interpreting the gonadotropin pattern.
items.answer: 685 pmol/L × 272.4 ÷ 1000 ≈ 187 pg/mL. At that concentration, estradiol should profoundly suppress LH and FSH. The boy's LH 2.4 and FSH 4.7 mIU/mL are prepubertal and not suppressed — physiologically incompatible with sustained E2 ~187 pg/mL — so the conversion shows the high value, if real, contradicts the gonadotropin pattern and is likely an immunoassay artefact pending LC-MS/MS confirmation.

items.question: Outline the three-step diagnostic hierarchy for this unresolved case.
items.answer: Step 1 — Verify: LC-MS/MS estradiol and testosterone on a fresh sample; meticulous exogenous-estrogen history in all caregivers; review May 3 panel (beta-hCG, DHEAS, LFT, thyroid). Step 2 — Adjudicate: bone-age X-ray (advanced BA confirms true hyperestrogenism; concordant BA supports artefact). Step 3 — Localise (only if E2 verified high): scrotal US, adrenal imaging if indicated, 17-OHP/steroid panel, CYP19A1 if aromatase-excess pattern; calculate free E2/T with SHBG. Treat by confirmed cause — never empiric therapy for an unverified number.
